questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who believes in fate and accepts all events as inevitable is called
questions.solution: A 'Fatalist' is one who believes that all events are predetermined and inevitable, accepting whatever happens as fate. 'Stoic' (A) refers to someone who endures pain without complaint — not specifically about fate. Students confuse Stoic with Fatalist because both imply acceptance, but Stoic is about emotional endurance, not belief in fate.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who hates mankind is known as a
questions.solution: A 'Misanthrope' hates or distrusts all of humankind. 'Misandrist' hates men specifically, 'Misogynist' hates women, and 'Philanderer' refers to someone who has casual romantic affairs. Students confuse Misanthrope with Misogynist, but '-anthrope' (Greek: human) vs '-gynist' (Greek: woman) clarifies the distinction.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who lives a life of strict self-denial and abstinence for spiritual reasons is a
questions.solution: An 'Ascetic' practices severe self-discipline and abstinence from worldly pleasures for spiritual reasons. 'Epicurean' and 'Hedonist' both seek pleasure — the opposite of ascetic. Students often confuse Ascetic with Stoic; an Ascetic actively denies pleasure, while a Stoic endures hardship without emotional reaction.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who is all powerful is called
questions.solution: 'Omnipotent' means all-powerful (Latin: omni = all, potent = powerful). 'Omniscient' = all-knowing, 'Omnipresent' = present everywhere, 'Omnivorous' = eating all types of food. Students confuse Omniscient with Omnipotent — the Latin roots '-scient' (knowing) and '-potent' (powerful) are the key differentiators.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who walks in sleep is a
questions.solution: A 'Somnambulist' is a sleepwalker (Latin: somnus = sleep, ambulare = to walk). 'Insomniac' cannot sleep, 'Hypochondriac' imagines being ill. Students frequently confuse Somnambulist with Insomniac — both relate to sleep disorders, but Insomniac cannot sleep while Somnambulist walks during sleep.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who is incapable of being corrected or reformed is
questions.solution: 'Incorrigible' describes a person too firmly fixed in bad habits to be reformed (Latin: in = not, corrigere = to correct). 'Invincible' = cannot be conquered, 'Infallible' = never wrong. Students confuse Incorrigible with Incurable; the former refers to behavioral reform, the latter to physical ailments.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person indifferent to pleasure or pain and who shows great self-control is
questions.solution: A 'Stoic' is someone who endures pain or hardship without complaint and with great self-control, derived from the ancient Greek philosophy of Stoicism. 'Cynic' distrusts human sincerity; 'Pacifist' opposes violence. Students confuse Stoic (Q1 answer Fatalist) — Stoic = self-control under adversity, Fatalist = acceptance of fate.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A speech delivered without any previous preparation is
questions.solution: 'Extempore' means spoken or done without preparation (Latin: ex tempore = out of the moment). 'Loquacious' and 'Garrulous' both mean talkative. Students confuse 'Extempore' with 'Eloquent' (well-spoken) — eloquence refers to quality of expression, not spontaneity.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who eats human flesh is called a
questions.solution: A 'Cannibal' is a person who eats human flesh. 'Carnivore' eats meat from animals, 'Herbivore' eats plants, 'Omnivore' eats both. Students confuse Cannibal with Carnivore — both eat meat, but Cannibal specifically refers to humans eating humans.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who rules without consulting anyone and exercises absolute power is a
questions.solution: An 'Autocrat' rules with absolute power without consulting others (Greek: autos = self, kratos = power). 'Democrat' = rule by the people; 'Aristocrat' = rule by nobility. Students confuse Autocrat with Aristocrat — '-crat' suffix is common, but 'auto-' (self) vs 'aristo-' (best/noble) distinguishes them.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who pretends to be what he is not is a
questions.solution: A 'Hypocrite' is someone who pretends to have virtues or beliefs they do not actually hold (Greek: hypokrites = stage actor, one who plays a part). 'Prodigy' = gifted person, 'Novice/Tyro' = beginner. Students confuse Hypocrite with Cynic — a Cynic distrusts others' sincerity, a Hypocrite is insincere themselves.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who is new to a job, activity or situation is a
questions.solution: A 'Novice' is someone new to or inexperienced in a field (Latin: novus = new). 'Connoisseur' = expert judge of quality; 'Veteran' = long-experienced person; 'Maestro' = master of an art. Students confuse Novice with Tyro — both mean beginners, but Novice is more commonly used in professional/religious contexts.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who can use both hands with equal ease is
questions.solution: 'Ambidextrous' describes the ability to use both hands equally well (Latin: ambi = both, dexter = right/skillful). 'Ambivalent' = mixed feelings; 'Ambiguous' = unclear meaning; 'Amphibious' = living on land and water. Students confuse Ambidextrous with Ambivalent — both begin with 'ambi-' but refer to very different concepts.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who hates marriage is called a
questions.solution: A 'Misogamist' is someone who hates or opposes marriage (Greek: misos = hatred, gamos = marriage). 'Bigamist' = married to two people simultaneously; 'Polygamist' = multiple spouses; 'Celibate' = abstains from sex/marriage by choice. Students confuse Misogamist with Celibate — a Celibate chooses not to marry, a Misogamist hates the institution.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who does a thing for pleasure and not as a profession is
questions.solution: An 'Amateur' does something for pleasure rather than as a paid profession (French: amateur = lover of). 'Novice' = beginner; 'Veteran' = experienced professional. Students confuse Amateur with Novice — an Amateur may be skilled but unpaid, while a Novice is specifically inexperienced regardless of payment.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who talks too much is
questions.solution: 'Garrulous' means excessively talkative, especially about trivial things (Latin: garrulus = chattering). 'Reticent', 'Taciturn', and 'Laconic' all mean speaking very little — the opposites of garrulous. Students must identify that A, B, C are all antonyms of the answer, making this a careful process of elimination.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who loves books is a
questions.solution: A 'Bibliophile' is a lover of books (Greek: biblion = book, philos = lover). 'Philatelist' collects stamps; 'Numismatist' collects coins; 'Linguist' studies languages. Students confuse Bibliophile with Philatelist — both begin with 'Phil-' (love) but 'biblio-' (book) vs 'atel-' (stamps) distinguishes them.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who collects stamps is a
questions.solution: A 'Philatelist' collects postage stamps (Greek: philos = lover, ateleia = tax-free, referring to prepaid postage). 'Numismatist' collects coins; 'Archaeologist' studies ancient history through excavation. Students confuse Philatelist with Philanderer — both begin with 'Phila-' but Philanderer means a man who has romantic affairs.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who loves mankind is a
questions.solution: A 'Philanthropist' loves and helps humanity through charitable acts (Greek: philos = loving, anthropos = human). 'Philologist' studies languages; 'Philosopher' studies fundamental questions. Students confuse Philanthropist with Philologist — 'anthropo-' (human) vs '-logy' (study of) is the distinguishing element.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who doubts the existence of God is
questions.solution: An 'Agnostic' believes the existence of God is unknown or unknowable (Greek: a = not, gnosis = knowledge). 'Atheist' firmly does not believe in God; 'Theist' believes in God. Students frequently confuse Agnostic with Atheist — Agnostic doubts/is unsure, Atheist is certain there is no God; doubt vs denial is the key distinction.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who is unable to sleep is called
questions.solution: An 'Insomniac' is someone who suffers from chronic inability to sleep (Latin: in = not, somnus = sleep). 'Somnambulist' walks in sleep; 'Hypochondriac' imagines illness. Students confuse Insomniac with Somnambulist — both relate to sleep but Insomniac cannot sleep while Somnambulist sleeps and walks.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who studies the origin and history of words is a
questions.solution: A 'Philologist' studies the history and structure of language and literary texts (Greek: philos = loving, logos = word/reason). 'Anthropologist' studies human societies; 'Archaeologist' studies ancient artifacts. Students confuse Philologist with Linguist — Philologist focuses on historical development of language, Linguist studies language structure more broadly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A government by a small group of powerful people is called
questions.solution: 'Oligarchy' is rule by a small group of people who hold power (Greek: oligos = few, arche = rule). 'Democracy' = rule by the people; 'Monarchy' = rule by one; 'Theocracy' = rule by religious authority. Students confuse Oligarchy with Aristocracy — both involve elite groups, but Aristocracy specifically refers to nobility by birth.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: One who is excessively fond of one's own self and looks is a
questions.solution: A 'Narcissist' is excessively self-admiring and preoccupied with their own appearance and importance (from Narcissus of Greek mythology). 'Egoist' is self-centred in thought/action; 'Masochist' derives pleasure from own pain. Students confuse Narcissist with Egoist — Narcissist specifically involves vanity and appearance, Egoist is broader self-centredness.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A person who deliberately sets fire to property is
questions.solution: An 'Arsonist' is a person who deliberately sets fire to property as a criminal act. 'Pyromaniac' has a compulsive urge to start fires (psychological disorder); 'Vandal' causes general destruction. Students confuse Arsonist with Pyromaniac — Arsonist is the legal/criminal term, Pyromaniac is the psychological/compulsive condition.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A judge's private room without the press or public present is called
questions.solution: 'In camera' (Latin: in private room) refers to legal proceedings held in private, away from press and public. 'Ante-chamber' = a waiting room; 'Cloister' = a covered walk in a monastery. Students may guess Ante-chamber (sounds official) but the Latin legal term 'in camera' is the precise one-word substitution.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A period of time in the past that was idyllically happy and peaceful is
questions.solution: 'Halcyon' describes a period of past happiness and prosperity (from the mythical bird halcyon that calmed the seas). 'Nostalgia' is the feeling of longing for the past; 'Utopia' is an ideal future society; 'Elysium' is the paradise of Greek mythology. Students confuse Halcyon with Nostalgic — Halcyon describes the period itself, Nostalgia describes the feeling about it.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A tax or levy is known as
questions.solution: A 'Tariff' is a tax or duty imposed on imported or exported goods. 'Cess' is a type of tax levied for a specific purpose; 'Duty' is a tax on imports/exports; 'Excise' is a tax on domestic goods. Students find this tricky as all four options are types of taxes — Tariff is the most general term for a tax/levy on trade.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An abbreviation formed from initial letters and pronounced as a word is
questions.solution: An 'Acronym' is a word formed from the initial letters of a phrase and pronounced as a word (e.g., NASA, NATO). 'Initialism' uses initial letters pronounced as separate letters (e.g., FBI, BBC). Students confuse Acronym with Initialism — the key distinction is that Acronyms are pronounced as words, Initialisms are spelled out letter by letter.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An ability to do several things at the same time is
questions.solution: 'Multitasking' is the ability to perform multiple tasks simultaneously. 'Versatility' = ability to adapt to many different functions; 'Coordination' = organization of elements working together. Students confuse Multitasking with Versatility — Versatility means capable of many things across different domains, Multitasking means doing several things at the same time.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An ability to express oneself well in speech is
questions.solution: 'Oracy' is the ability to express oneself fluently and grammatically in speech (coined by Andrew Wilkinson, 1965). 'Eloquence' = persuasive and fluent expression; 'Rhetoric' = art of effective speaking/writing; 'Diction' = choice and use of words. Students tend to choose Eloquence, but Oracy is the precise one-word substitution for ability in spoken expression.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An ability to make good judgments and take quick decisions shows
questions.solution: 'Acumen' is the ability to make good judgments and quick decisions in a particular domain (Latin: acuere = to sharpen). 'Prudence' = careful in decision-making over time; 'Foresight' = seeing future consequences; 'Discernment' = good judgment perceptively. Students confuse Acumen with Prudence — Acumen implies sharpness and quickness, Prudence implies caution and deliberation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An ability to see what will happen in the future is
questions.solution: 'Foresight' is the ability to predict or plan for future events (Old English: foresihth = ability to see ahead). 'Prescience' = knowing things before they happen; 'Clairvoyance' = supernatural ability to see the future; 'Prognosis' = medical prediction of illness outcome. Students confuse Foresight with Prescience — Foresight is a natural skill, Prescience implies supernatural or divine foreknowledge.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An ability to stay calm in difficult situations is
questions.solution: 'Sangfroid' is the ability to remain calm in difficult situations (French: sang froid = cold blood). 'Equanimity' = mental calmness; 'Composure' = self-control of feelings; 'Poise' = graceful calmness. Students find this difficult as all four mean calmness — Sangfroid specifically implies coolness under pressure/danger, making it the most precise one-word substitution.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An ability to use hands skillfully or think quickly is
questions.solution: 'Dexterity' is skill and ease in using one's hands, or mental skill in handling situations (Latin: dexter = right hand = skillful). 'Adroitness' = cleverness or skill; 'Prowess' = skill in a specific activity. Students confuse Dexterity with Adroitness — Dexterity specifically covers physical (hand) and mental quickness, while Adroitness is more broadly clever/skillful.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An ability, talent, or special skill needed to do something is a
questions.solution: A 'Knack' is a special skill or talent for doing something easily (Middle English: knakke = clever trick). 'Flair' = natural talent with style; 'Aptitude' = natural ability to learn; 'Gift' = innate talent. Students confuse Knack with Flair — Knack implies ease/trick in doing something specific, Flair implies a stylish natural talent.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Able to become strong after adversity describes something
questions.solution: 'Resilient' describes the ability to recover quickly from difficulties (Latin: resilire = to spring back). 'Tenacious' = holding firm despite opposition; 'Robust' = strong and healthy; 'Buoyant' = cheerful and optimistic. Students confuse Resilient with Tenacious — Resilient = bouncing back after adversity, Tenacious = holding on stubbornly despite adversity.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Able to do many different things characterizes a
questions.solution: 'Versatile' describes the ability to adapt or be used for many different purposes (Latin: versatilis = turning easily). 'Polymath' = a person of wide knowledge; 'Adaptable' = able to adjust to new conditions; 'Eclectic' (note: 'Ecletric' in paper is a misspelling) = selecting from various sources. Students confuse Versatile with Polymath — Polymath has wide knowledge, Versatile has wide practical ability.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Able to exist together without conflict is
questions.solution: 'Compatible' means able to exist or work together without conflict (Latin: compati = to suffer with). 'Harmonious' = free from conflict; 'Symbiotic' = mutually beneficial relationship; 'Congruent' = in agreement/matching. Students confuse Compatible with Harmonious — Compatible implies the fundamental ability to coexist, Harmonious implies the pleasant quality of that coexistence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Acceptable according to law or reasonable is
questions.solution: 'Legitimate' means conforming to the law or rules, or reasonable and acceptable (Latin: legitimus = lawful). 'Bona fide' = genuine/authentic; 'Valid' = legally or logically sound. Students confuse Legitimate with Valid — Valid means acceptable in logic/argument, Legitimate specifically means lawfully or morally acceptable.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Accepting traditional religious beliefs strictly is
questions.solution: 'Orthodox' means adhering to traditional, established forms of a religion or philosophy (Greek: orthos = right, doxa = opinion/belief). 'Fundamentalist' = strict literal adherence to core texts; 'Devout' = sincerely religious. Students confuse Orthodox with Fundamentalist — Orthodox means following traditional doctrine, Fundamentalist implies extreme literalism and intolerance of deviation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of burying a dead person is
questions.solution: 'Interment' is the formal act of burying a dead body in a grave (Latin: inter = into the earth). 'Inhumation' also means burial but is more technical/archaeological. 'Cremation' = burning the body. Students confuse Interment with Inhumation — both mean burial, but Interment is the more common one-word substitution for the formal burial act.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of carefully watching to prevent crime is
questions.solution: 'Surveillance' is close observation, especially by authorities, to prevent or detect crime (French: surveiller = to watch over). 'Vigilance' = the state of being watchful; 'Espionage' = spying for another country; 'Reconnaissance' = military scouting. Students confuse Surveillance with Vigilance — Surveillance is systematic monitoring/watching, Vigilance is the quality/state of being alert.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act causing two parties to become friendly after disagreement is
questions.solution: 'Reconciliation' is the act of restoring friendly relations after a disagreement (Latin: reconciliare = to make friendly again). 'Mediation' = intervention by a third party; 'Detente' = easing of hostility between nations; 'Amity' = a state of friendly relations. Students confuse Reconciliation with Mediation — Mediation is the process using a third party, Reconciliation is the outcome of restored friendship.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of committing a serious crime like murder is a
questions.solution: A 'Felony' is a serious crime such as murder, rape, or armed robbery, typically carrying severe penalties (Old French: felonie = wickedness). 'Misdemeanor' = a minor wrongdoing; 'Transgression' = violation of a rule. Students confuse Felony with Offense — Offense is a general term for any crime/wrongdoing, Felony specifically refers to serious crimes in legal classification.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of destroying something deliberately to impair function is
questions.solution: 'Sabotage' is deliberate action aimed at weakening an organization or effort, especially by damaging or obstructing it (French: saboter = to clatter with sabots/shoes). 'Vandalism' = deliberate destruction of property; 'Demolition' = systematic tearing down. Students confuse Sabotage with Vandalism — Sabotage targets functionality/operation, Vandalism targets property appearance/existence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of ending employment of workers is
questions.solution: 'Retrenchment' is the act of reducing costs by dismissing workers, especially during economic difficulty (French: retrancher = to cut back). 'Dismissal' = termination for cause; 'Layoff' = temporary or permanent suspension; 'Severance' = the amount paid when employment ends. Students confuse Retrenchment with Layoff — Retrenchment implies a deliberate policy of cost-cutting, Layoff can be temporary or for various reasons.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of explaining something is an
questions.solution: 'Exposition' is a comprehensive explanation or description (Latin: exponere = to set out/explain). 'Elucidation' = making something clear; 'Narration' = telling a story; 'Discourse' = written/spoken communication on a topic. Students confuse Exposition with Elucidation — Exposition is systematic comprehensive explanation, Elucidation is specifically about making something obscure clear.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of getting money back from payments made is
questions.solution: 'Reimbursement' is the act of compensating someone for expenses or losses (Latin: re = back, im = into, bursa = purse). 'Refund' = returning money paid; 'Clawback' = recovering money previously paid out. Students confuse Reimbursement with Refund — Refund is returning money for a product/service, Reimbursement is paying back expenses already incurred by another.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of travelling by a different route is a
questions.solution: A 'Detour' is a long or roundabout route taken to avoid something or as an excursion (French: détourner = to turn away). 'Bypass' = a road passing around a congested area; 'Diversion' = redirection of traffic; 'Deviation' = departing from a standard. Students confuse Detour with Diversion — Detour is deliberately taking an alternative route, Diversion is a redirection set up by others.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of identifying a disease by examination is
questions.solution: 'Diagnosis' is the identification of the nature of an illness by examination of symptoms (Greek: dia = through, gnosis = knowledge). 'Prognosis' = prediction of the likely course of a disease; 'Analysis' = detailed examination. Students confuse Diagnosis with Prognosis — Diagnosis identifies what the disease is, Prognosis predicts how the disease will progress.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of illegally copying products is
questions.solution: 'Piracy' is the unauthorized use or reproduction of another's work, especially digital or media content (Greek: peiratēs = brigand). 'Plagiarism' = copying written work presenting it as one's own; 'Counterfeiting' = making fake currency/goods; 'Bootlegging' = illegal copying/selling of recordings. Students confuse Piracy with Counterfeiting — Piracy focuses on copying/distribution rights, Counterfeiting focuses on faking physical goods or currency.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of neglecting duty is
questions.solution: 'Dereliction' is the shameful failure to fulfil one's obligations or duties (Latin: derelictus = abandoned). 'Negligence' = failure to take proper care; 'Oversight' = unintentional failure to notice something; 'Lapse' = a temporary failure. Students confuse Dereliction with Negligence — Dereliction implies deliberate or shameful abandonment of duty, Negligence is unintentional carelessness.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of officially charging with a crime is an
questions.solution: An 'Indictment' is a formal charge or accusation of a serious crime (Latin: in + dicere = to proclaim). 'Arraignment' = formal reading of charges in court; 'Impeachment' = charging a public official with misconduct; 'Accusation' = a claim that someone has done something wrong. Students confuse Indictment with Arraignment — Indictment is the formal document charging the crime, Arraignment is the court appearance where charges are read.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of printing false damaging statements is
questions.solution: 'Libel' is the publication of false statements that damage a person's reputation specifically in written/printed form (Latin: libellus = little book). 'Slander' = spoken false statements; 'Defamation' = general term for damaging reputation; 'Calumny' = making false and damaging statements. Students confuse Libel with Slander — the key distinction is medium: Libel = printed/published, Slander = spoken.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of punishing in return for harm done is
questions.solution: 'Retribution' is punishment inflicted on someone as vengeance for a wrong or criminal act, often seen as deserved (Latin: retribuere = to pay back). 'Revenge' and 'Vengeance' are personal acts; 'Reprisal' = act in retaliation by a state/military. Students find this difficult as all options mean punishment-in-return — Retribution carries the sense of deserved, just punishment rather than personal emotion.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of remembering past experiences is
questions.solution: 'Reminiscence' is the act of indulging in enjoyable recollection of past events (Latin: reminisci = to remember). 'Recollection' = the act of remembering; 'Reflection' = serious thought/consideration; 'Nostalgia' = sentimental longing for the past. Students confuse Reminiscence with Recollection — Reminiscence implies a pleasant, indulgent act of remembering, Recollection is the neutral cognitive act of recall.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of returning lost or stolen property is
questions.solution: 'Restitution' is the act of returning property to its rightful owner or restoring a situation to its original state (Latin: restituere = to rebuild/restore). 'Compensation' = money paid for loss; 'Indemnity' = security against damage/loss; 'Reparation' = making amends for wrong. Students confuse Restitution with Reparation — Restitution specifically returns the original property/situation, Reparation makes amends but not necessarily by returning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of sending for treatment or advice is a
questions.solution: A 'Referral' is the act of directing a patient or client to a specialist or authority for treatment or advice (Latin: referre = to bring back). 'Recommendation' = a suggestion about the best course; 'Delegation' = entrusting responsibility to another; 'Remission' = a temporary recovery. Students confuse Referral with Recommendation — a Referral is a formal act of directing someone to a specific person/service, a Recommendation is a general suggestion.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of disrespecting holy things is
questions.solution: 'Sacrilege' is the violation or misuse of what is regarded as sacred (Latin: sacrilegus = one who steals sacred things). 'Blasphemy' = speaking irreverently about God; 'Heresy' = belief contrary to religious doctrine; 'Profanity' = irreverent language about sacred things. Students confuse Sacrilege with Blasphemy — Sacrilege involves physical desecration of sacred objects/places, Blasphemy specifically involves disrespectful speech about God.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act trying to impress by name-dropping is
questions.solution: 'Name-dropping' is the act of casually mentioning the names of famous people one knows in order to impress others. 'Bragging' and 'Boasting' are general self-praise; 'Vaunting' = boastful or proud. This question has an obvious answer as 'name-dropping' is explicitly the one-word substitution for the given definition — students should recognize it directly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act of romantic relationship outside marriage is
questions.solution: 'Adultery' is voluntary sexual intercourse between a married person and a person who is not their spouse (Latin: adulterare = to corrupt). 'Infidelity' = unfaithfulness in marriage; 'Affair' = a romantic/sexual relationship; 'Liaison' = a sexual relationship. Students confuse Adultery with Infidelity — Adultery is specifically the physical act of sexual intercourse outside marriage; Infidelity is the broader concept of unfaithfulness.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act sealing off to prevent entry or exit is a
questions.solution: A 'Blockade' is an act of sealing off a place to prevent goods, people or information from entering or leaving (block + -ade). 'Embargo' = official ban on trade; 'Quarantine' = isolation to prevent disease spread; 'Siege' = a military operation surrounding a place. Students confuse Blockade with Siege — a Siege involves surrounding with military force to compel surrender, a Blockade specifically prevents entry/exit.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act or remark to gain advantage is a
questions.solution: A 'Gambit' is an act or remark that is calculated to gain an advantage (from chess: opening move sacrificing a piece for strategic advantage). 'Ploy' = a cunning plan; 'Ruse' = a trick; 'Stratagem' = a plan to outwit an enemy. Students confuse Gambit with Stratagem — Gambit implies a calculated opening move accepting some risk, Stratagem is a broader military/competitive plan.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An act part of a religious ceremony is a
questions.solution: A 'Rite' is a social or religious ceremony of a customary nature (Latin: ritus = religious custom). 'Ritual' = a prescribed order of ceremonies; 'Sacrament' = a Christian ceremony of divine grace; 'Liturgy' = a form of public worship. Students confuse Rite with Ritual — a Rite is a specific act within a ceremony, a Ritual is the entire prescribed sequence of acts.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An action making amends for wrong is
questions.solution: 'Atonement' is the action of making amends for a wrong or injury (at-one-ment = becoming reconciled). 'Penance' = punishment self-imposed to show repentance; 'Expiation' = making amends through suffering; 'Propitiation' = winning favour by appeasing. Students confuse Atonement with Expiation — Atonement is the general act of making amends and reconciling, Expiation specifically involves suffering to make amends.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An action marrying superior caste is
questions.solution: 'Hypergamy' is the practice of marrying into an equal or higher social group or caste (Greek: hyper = above, gamos = marriage). 'Endogamy' = marrying within one's social group; 'Exogamy' = marrying outside one's social group; 'Anuloma' = marriage of higher caste male with lower caste female. Students confuse Hypergamy with Endogamy — Endogamy = marrying within the group, Hypergamy = marrying upward in hierarchy.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An action regaining possession by payment is
questions.solution: 'Redemption' is the action of regaining or gaining possession of something in exchange for payment (Latin: redimere = to buy back). 'Ransom' = a sum demanded for the release of a captive; 'Recovery' = regaining possession generally; 'Retrieval' = getting something back. Students confuse Redemption with Ransom — Redemption is a legitimate act of payment to reclaim something, Ransom is paid under duress/coercion.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: An action signaling to do something is a
questions.solution: A 'Cue' is a signal to begin or do something, especially in a theatrical or broadcasting context (possibly Latin: quando = when). 'Prompt' = a reminder or assist for an actor; 'Hint' = a small piece of indirect information. Students confuse Cue with Prompt — in theatrical terms, both help a performer, but Cue is the signal to start an action, Prompt is a reminder of words when forgotten.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Speaking sacrilegiously about God is
questions.solution: 'Blasphemy' is the act or offense of speaking sacrilegiously about God or sacred things (Greek: blasphemein = to speak ill of). 'Heresy' = belief contrary to orthodox doctrine; 'Apostasy' = abandonment of religious faith; 'Sacrilege' = violation of sacred things. Students confuse Blasphemy with Sacrilege — Blasphemy is specifically spoken/written disrespect toward God, Sacrilege involves physical desecration of sacred objects.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Art of copying behavior or speech is
questions.solution: 'Mimicry' is the close imitation of another person's behavior, speech, or expressions (Greek: mimikos = imitative). 'Imitation' = general copying; 'Caricature' = an exaggerated representation; 'Parody' = comic imitation of a style/work. Students confuse Mimicry with Imitation — Mimicry specifically refers to the art/skill of copying behaviors and sounds, Imitation is the broader general act.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Unwanted unusual activity or excitement is a
questions.solution: 'Commotion' is a state of confused and noisy disturbance (Latin: commovere = to disturb greatly). 'Fuss' = unnecessary excitement over something trivial; 'Uproar' = a loud and impassioned noise; 'Turmoil' = a state of great disturbance/confusion. Students confuse Commotion with Uproar — Commotion implies confused disturbance, Uproar specifically implies loud, angry noise.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Activity gaining popularity is jumping on the
questions.solution: The 'Bandwagon' refers to a popular trend or activity that people join because others are doing it (from the literal bandwagon in parades that people would jump onto). 'Trend' = general direction of change; 'Craze/Fad' = a brief intense enthusiasm. Students confuse Bandwagon with Fad — Bandwagon emphasizes joining because others are doing it (social pressure), Fad emphasizes the transient nature of the popularity.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Actor or actress is a
questions.solution: A 'Thespian' is an actor or actress (from Thespis, the ancient Greek poet considered the first actor). 'Artiste' = a professional entertainer; 'Performer' = general term for one who performs. Students confuse Thespian with Artiste — Thespian specifically refers to theatrical actors, Artiste is a broader term for any professional entertainer.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Add notes to text for explanation is to
questions.solution: To 'Annotate' is to add notes to a text or diagram giving explanation or comment (Latin: annotare = to note down). 'Footnote' = a note at the bottom of a page; 'Gloss' = a brief explanation; 'Comment' = a verbal or written remark. Students confuse Annotate with Gloss — Annotate involves systematically adding notes throughout a text, Gloss provides a brief marginal or interlinear explanation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Additional info after main story is
questions.solution: An 'Addendum' is an item of additional material added at the end of a book or other publication (Latin: addendum = thing to be added). 'Postscript' = note added after letter's signature; 'Appendix' = supplementary material at end of book; 'Codicil' = addition to a will. Students confuse Addendum with Appendix — Appendix contains supplementary supporting material, Addendum adds new information not in the original.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Gains from corrupt practices are
questions.solution: 'Graft' refers to money obtained through dishonest or corrupt practices, especially in politics (possibly from the idea of grafting/inserting oneself for gain). 'Bribe' = money offered to influence judgment; 'Emolument' = a salary/fee; 'Perquisite' = a benefit/advantage. Students confuse Graft with Bribe — Bribe is money given to corrupt a specific person/decision, Graft is the broader term for systematic corrupt gain.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Advice based on birth date and stars is
questions.solution: A 'Horoscope' is a forecast of a person's future based on the relative positions of stars and planets at their time of birth (Greek: hora = time, skopos = observer). 'Astrology' = the study/practice of reading stars' influence; 'Palmistry' = reading palms; 'Numerology' = using numbers. Students confuse Horoscope with Astrology — Astrology is the practice/field, Horoscope is the specific forecast/advice produced by astrology.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Agreed to by everyone is
questions.solution: 'Unanimous' means fully agreed by all people (Latin: unus = one, animus = mind). 'Consensus' = general agreement; 'Concordant' = in agreement; 'Harmonious' = free from conflict. Students confuse Unanimous with Consensus — Unanimous means every single person agrees (100%), Consensus means general agreement with possible exceptions.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Agreement to stop fighting temporarily is a
questions.solution: An 'Armistice' is a formal agreement between opposing parties to stop fighting while a peace treaty is negotiated (Latin: arma = arms, sistere = to stop). 'Ceasefire' = an informal/immediate halt; 'Truce' = an agreement to stop temporarily; 'Pacification' = the process of making peaceful. Students confuse Armistice with Truce — Armistice is a formal official agreement to stop war, Truce is a less formal temporary halt.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Cheap illegal alcoholic liquor is
questions.solution: 'Moonshine' is illegally distilled alcohol, originally made secretly at night by moonlight (American English). 'Hooch' = low-quality alcoholic drink; 'Bootleg' = illegally made/distributed goods; 'Spirits' = strong distilled alcohol generally. Students confuse Moonshine with Hooch — Moonshine specifically refers to illegally distilled spirits, Hooch is a more general slang for cheap/inferior alcohol.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: All plants in a particular area are the
questions.solution: 'Flora' refers to all the plants of a particular region, habitat, or geological period (from Flora, Roman goddess of flowers). 'Vegetation' = plants collectively in an area; 'Foliage' = leaves of plants collectively; 'Greenery' = green plants. Students confuse Flora with Vegetation — Flora is the scientific/taxonomic term for all plant species in an area, Vegetation refers to the plant cover generally.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: All things a person can do form their
questions.solution: A 'Repertoire' is the complete range of skills or works that a performer, company, or person is able to perform (French: répertoire = list/catalogue). 'Arsenal' = a collection of weapons or resources; 'Toolkit' = a set of tools. Students confuse Repertoire with Arsenal — Repertoire refers to a person's range of abilities/performances, Arsenal refers to a stockpile of resources/weapons (used metaphorically for a large collection).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Allowed to know a secret is
questions.solution: 'Privy' (to) means sharing in the knowledge of something secret (Latin: privatus = private). 'Confidant' = a person trusted with secrets (a noun, not the state of knowing); 'Informed' = having information. Students confuse Privy with Confidant — Privy describes the state of being allowed to know a secret, Confidant is the person with whom secrets are shared.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Always happening or existing is
questions.solution: 'Persistent' means continuing firmly or obstinately despite obstacles; always continuing to happen (Latin: persistere = to stand firm). 'Chronic' = persisting for a long time (especially of illness); 'Inveterate' = long-established habit; 'Habitual' = done regularly as a habit. Students confuse Persistent with Chronic — Persistent emphasizes continuous occurrence despite opposition, Chronic specifically refers to long-standing conditions (usually medical).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Amount more than needed is
questions.solution: A 'Surplus' is an amount of something left over when requirements have been met; more than what is needed (French: surplus = over and above). 'Excess' = going beyond normal limits; 'Abundance' = a very large quantity; 'Glut' = an excessively abundant supply. Students confuse Surplus with Excess — Surplus specifically means more than what is required/needed, Excess means beyond what is normal/permitted.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money to retiring employee is
questions.solution: 'Gratuity' is a sum of money paid to an employee upon retirement as recognition of service (Latin: gratuitas = a free gift). 'Bonus' = extra payment for good performance; 'Stipend' = a fixed regular payment; 'Allowance' = money given for a specific purpose. Students confuse Gratuity with Bonus — Gratuity is paid upon retirement/leaving as a token of service, Bonus is paid during employment for performance.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money paid for service is
questions.solution: An 'Honorarium' is a payment given for professional services rendered voluntarily or for which fees are not legally required (Latin: honorarium = relating to honor). 'Fee' = a payment for professional advice; 'Remuneration' = payment for work done; 'Wage' = regular payment by employer. Students confuse Honorarium with Fee — Honorarium is given as an honor/courtesy for services not legally requiring payment, Fee is the standard charge for a professional service.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money to old or sick non-workers is
questions.solution: A 'Pension' is a regular payment made to retired, elderly, or disabled persons (Latin: pensio = a payment). 'Subsidy' = money given by government to support a business; 'Benefit' = a payment to which someone is entitled; 'Allowance' = money given for a specific purpose. Students confuse Pension with Benefit — Pension is specifically for retired/aged/disabled non-workers, Benefit is a broader government payment for various needs.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money for help in dishonest deal is
questions.solution: A 'Kickback' is a payment made to someone for facilitating a transaction or appointment, especially as part of a corrupt arrangement (kick + back = returning part of payment). 'Bribe' = money to influence judgment; 'Hush money' = paid to ensure silence; 'Payoff' = a bribe or financial benefit. Students confuse Kickback with Bribe — Kickback specifically involves a return payment to one who facilitated a deal, Bribe is paid upfront to corrupt a decision.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money paid at one time is a
questions.solution: A 'Lump sum' is a single payment made at one time rather than in installments (lump = compact mass + sum = amount). 'Installment' = one of several payments; 'Annuity' = a fixed annual payment; 'Down payment' = initial partial payment. Students confuse Lump sum with Down payment — Down payment is a partial first payment with more to follow, Lump sum is the complete amount paid at once.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money spent is an
questions.solution: 'Expenditure' is the action of spending funds; money spent (Latin: expendere = to weigh out/pay). 'Disbursement' = paying out money from a fund; 'Outlay' = an amount of money spent; 'Cost' = the amount paid to acquire something. Students confuse Expenditure with Outlay — Expenditure is the general comprehensive term for money spent, Outlay implies a specific capital spending for investment/setup.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money collected by authority is a
questions.solution: A 'Levy' is a tax or other compulsory payment collected by an authority (French: levée = raising). 'Tax' = compulsory payment to government; 'Toll' = payment to use a road/bridge; 'Fine' = payment as a penalty. Students confuse Levy with Tax — Levy is the act of collecting or the general term for compulsory collection, Tax is the specific government-imposed charge on income/goods.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Amount produced is
questions.solution: 'Yield' is the amount of something produced (especially agricultural or industrial output) (Old English: gieldan = to pay). 'Output' = general term for production; 'Produce' = fresh food/agricultural products; 'Product' = something manufactured. Students confuse Yield with Output — Yield specifically refers to the amount produced relative to input (especially agriculture/investment), Output is the general quantity produced.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Time holding job or title is
questions.solution: 'Tenure' is the holding of an office, the conditions under which it is held, or the period of such holding (Latin: tenere = to hold). 'Incumbency' = the holding of an office or position; 'Term' = a fixed period; 'Duration' = the time something lasts. Students confuse Tenure with Incumbency — Tenure refers to the holding conditions and period of any position, Incumbency specifically refers to a current holder's period in office.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Amusing and very strange is
questions.solution: 'Wacky' means amusing and very strange or unusual in behavior (origin uncertain, possibly from 'whack'). 'Bizarre' = very strange or unusual; 'Eccentric' = unconventional in behavior; 'Whimsical' = playfully quaint. Students confuse Wacky with Bizarre — Bizarre implies strangeness that may be unsettling, Wacky implies strangeness that is amusing and entertaining.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Angry unpleasant woman is a
questions.solution: A 'Shrew' is a bad-tempered or aggressively assertive woman (from Shakespeare's 'The Taming of the Shrew'; originally a small aggressive mammal). 'Harpy' = a grasping, unpleasant woman; 'Termagant' = a harsh-tempered woman; 'Vixen' = a female fox, or a quarrelsome woman. Students find this difficult as all four refer to unpleasant women — Shrew specifically emphasizes bad-temperedness and scolding nature.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Complicated long argument is a
questions.solution: A 'Wrangle' is a long, complicated dispute or argument (Low German: wrangeln = to wrestle). 'Dispute' = a disagreement; 'Altercation' = a noisy argument; 'Quarrel' = an angry disagreement. Students confuse Wrangle with Altercation — Altercation implies a noisy public confrontation, Wrangle implies a prolonged, complicated, and exhausting argument.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Invigilator at university exam is a
questions.solution: A 'Proctor' is a person who invigilates (supervises) students during an examination, especially at a university (Latin: procurator = a person acting for another). 'Invigilator' = supervisor during exam; 'Warden' = a guardian/official. Students confuse Proctor with Invigilator — both supervise exams, but Proctor is the specific university/formal term, Invigilator is the more general British English term.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Ancient text like book of medicines is a
questions.solution: A 'Codex' is an ancient manuscript text, especially in book form (Latin: codex = tree trunk/wooden tablet, later a bound book). 'Manuscript' = a handwritten text; 'Scroll' = a roll of papyrus/parchment; 'Tome' = a large heavy book. Students confuse Codex with Manuscript — a Manuscript is any handwritten document, a Codex is specifically an ancient book-form manuscript with bound pages as opposed to a scroll.
